coredata.dc:title: Improvement of electrocatalytic performance in MnFeCoNiCu2 high-entropy alloy for urea synthesis by introducing functional graphene quantum dot and copper-rich phase

coredata.dc:description: High entropy alloy become an ideal candidate of electrocatalysts because of excellent conductivity, multifunctionality, selectivity and stability. However, poor catalytic performance restricts its wide applications in industrial production. The paper reports one strategy for improvement of electrocatalytic performance in MnFeCoNiCu2 high-entropy alloy (HEA) nanoparticles for urea synthesis by introducing serine and histidine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (SHB-GQD) and copper-rich phase. The study reveals strong coordination between SHB-GQD and metal ion ensures an uniform distribution of all metal elements at atom level in solution and solid states. The explosion caused by thermal decomposition of SHB-GQD and cotton at high temperature achieves to mild atomization and alloying. The resulted MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA shows an irregular spherical shape with small particle size of 125 ± 1.5 nm, double FCC phase and good element homogeneity. The introduction of SHB-GQD and copper-rich phase realizes a significantly improved electrocatalytic activity for urea synthesis. However, the improvement depends on structure of GQD. This gives us one new insight to make various HEA nanomaterials with a desired catalytic performance by rational design of GQD. MnFeCoNiCu2HEA/SHB-GQD electrocatalyst exhibits high urea yield of 59.01 mmol g−1 h−1 with high Faradaic efficiency of 26.6 %, which is more than that of reported electrocatalysts.

Structural model of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD (A), free energies for adsorption of CO2, NO3 - and H+ (B), free energy profiles of CO2 reduction to *CO (C) and free energy profiles of NO3 - reduction to *NH2 on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD (D), and free energy profile of C-N coupling on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD (E).
Electrocatalytic performance of different catalysts for urea synthesis.

head.ce:title: Improvement of electrocatalytic performance in MnFeCoNiCu2 high-entropy alloy for urea synthesis by introducing functional graphene quantum dot and copper-rich phase

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Science and Health Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High entropy alloy become an ideal candidate of electrocatalysts because of excellent conductivity, multifunctionality, selectivity and stability. However, poor catalytic performance restricts its wide applications in industrial production. The paper reports one strategy for improvement of electrocatalytic performance in MnFeCoNiCu2 high-entropy alloy (HEA) nanoparticles for urea synthesis by introducing serine and histidine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (SHB-GQD) and copper-rich phase. The study reveals strong coordination between SHB-GQD and metal ion ensures an uniform distribution of all metal elements at atom level in solution and solid states. The explosion caused by thermal decomposition of SHB-GQD and cotton at high temperature achieves to mild atomization and alloying. The resulted MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA shows an irregular spherical shape with small particle size of 125 ± 1.5 nm, double FCC phase and good element homogeneity. The introduction of SHB-GQD and copper-rich phase realizes a significantly improved electrocatalytic activity for urea synthesis. However, the improvement depends on structure of GQD. This gives us one new insight to make various HEA nanomaterials with a desired catalytic performance by rational design of GQD. MnFeCoNiCu2HEA/SHB-GQD electrocatalyst exhibits high urea yield of 59.01 mmol g−1 h−1 with high Faradaic efficiency of 26.6 %, which is more than that of reported electrocatalysts.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: We firstly reported one new way for improvement of electrocatalytic performance in MnFeCoNiCu2 high-entropy alloy for urea synthesis by introducing functional graphene quantum dot and copper-rich phase.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: High entropy alloy (HEA) is one kind of multicomponent alloy composing of five or more metal elements with a near equimolar ratio [1]. Due to the accumulation of entropy, HEA is a stable in a form of single-phase solid solution. The characteristic makes all elements can be fully mixed to form different multi-element active sites. By the choice of element configuration and composition, these active sites can be optimized for specific purposes [2]. Accordingly, HEA shows a prominent potential in electrocatalyst, including hydrogen evolution [3], oxygen reduction [4], oxygen evolution [5], CO2 reduction [6], NH3 decomposition [7], urea synthesis [8], and energy storage and conversion [9]. In contrast to bulk HEA, HEA nanoparticle is more suitable for use as an electrocatalyst because of a much larger surface area [10]. To date, HEA nanoparticle has become an ideal candidate of electrocatalysts because of excellent conductivity, multifunctionality, selectivity and structural stability. However, poor electrocatalytic performance restricts its applications in industrial production.

Many approaches have been investigated for the preparation of HEA nanoparticles to improve the catalytic performance. These approaches can be generally divided into physical route and wet chemical route. The physical route utilizes physical energy to achieve the dispersion, reduction and alloying of metal elements. The commonly used physical routes include carbothermal shock method [11], fast moving bed pyrolysis [12] and mechanical alloying method [13]. Carbothermal shock method is to achieve the alloying of metal elements on the carbon support by alternately heating and cooling. Typically, the heating adopts a high temperature of close to 3000 K with a duration time of milliseconds. The use of such a high temperature can ensure the full conversion of metal salt into atom vapors. Their effective collision in space leads to the alloying of metal elements under high entropy tendency. The cooling adopts a high cooling rate of > 100 K s−1 to avoid the segregation of metal atoms [14]. However, the carbothermal shock method for preparation of HEA with single-phase structure needs the use of special equipment and carefully designed process parameters. Similar with the carbothermal shock method, the fast-moving bed pyrolysis realizes the formation of HEA by low free energy driving nuclei formation at high temperature. The process achieves to a sequential conversion from metal precursors to monomer and nucleus and finally forms nanocrystal [15]. By altering the radius of nuclei and free energy in nucleation process, the size and composition of HEA nanocrystals can be effectively controlled. However, the achievement of this goal strongly requires the elaborate design of reaction temperature and nucleation rate [16]. Different from carbothermal shock method and fast-moving bed pyrolysis, the mechanical alloying method includes mechanical grinding and thermal treatment [17]. Firstly, the metal elemental casting is transformed into fine powder (embryo body) via mechanical grinding. Then, the resulted embryo body was changed into HEA nanoparticles by thermal treatment. However, the entire operation needs to be completed in an inert atmosphere to avoid the oxidation of metal elements [18]. Compared with physical route, the wet-chemistry route offers three advantages for preparation of HEA nanoparticles. The first advantage is that wet chemical route has a better adaptability. The most of transition metal precursors can be easily introduced to generate a desired HEA [19]. The second advantage is that the growth process of HEA nanocrystals can be adjusted by controlling reaction conditions such as temperature, time, reducing agent, surfactant and ligand [20]. The third advantage is that wet chemical route adopts a relatively low temperature to make HEA nanoparticles [21]. However, the wet-chemistry method as a near-equilibrium approach also exists some limitations for the preparation of HEA nanoparticles with strongly immiscible elements, which may cause a serious phase separation [22]. To overcome the problem, some wet chemical methods have been studied for the preparation of HEA nanoparticles, such as electrodeposited method [23] and solvothermal method [24]. Electrodeposited method uses electrical energy to realize rapid reduction and alloying of metal precursors at the electrode/electrolyte interface. The method is simple, effective and low-energy. The formed HEA nanocrystals show a small size of several nanometers. However, this method is not suitable for the preparation of some HEA nanoparticles containing metals that are difficult to be reduced [25]. Different from the electrodeposited method, the solvothermal method carries out the alloying of metal precursors in the bulk solution [26]. Thus, it is easier to use for industrial production of HEA nanoparticles. Often, the solvothermal method includes the dispersion of metal salts in a gel solution, drying to remove the solvent, and thermal annealing to finish the reduction and alloying of metal precursors. Recently, the solvothermal method received a more attention in the synthesis of HEA. To speed up the solvent molecule moving, the physical energies have been used for the solvothermal method, such as microwave [27] and ultrasound [28]. In addition, some functional molecules were also used for achieving to the above goal, including organic solvent [29] and chelator [30]. However, the practical impact of these efforts is not very ideal, because the chemical environment of metal elements has significantly changed during the drying and annealing. This leads to a poor structural homogeneity. At present, the solvothermal method is mainly limited to prepare the precious metal-based HEAs due to the weak reduction ability of organic solvent and common reducing agent. Despite these advances, scalable synthesis of HEA nanoparticles still faces some challenges. (1) The current methods are difficult to be well applied in the industrial production of HEA nanoparticles, because of the need to use special equipment and no desirable economic and environmental feasibility. (2) The current methods are difficult to be used for synthesis of HEA nanoparticles with single-phase structure nanoparticles, because the problem of structural heterogeneity is not full addressed. (3) The HEA nanoparticle lacks of an ideal electrocatalytic performance due to large particle size and unreasonable elemental composition.

In the study, we reported one strategy for improvement of electrocatalytic performance in MnFeCoNiCu2 high-entropy alloy (MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD) for urea synthesis by introducing serine and histidine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (SHB-GQD) and copper-rich phase. The strong coordination between SHB-GQD and metal ion ensures uniform distribution of all metal elements at atom level in a solution state and in a solid state and leads to the formation of HEA with single phase structure. The integration of SHB-GQD with copper-rich phase achieves to a significantly improved electrocatalytic performance. The MnFeCoNiCu2.0-HEA/SHB-GQD electrocatalyst exhibits high urea yield of 59.01 mmol g−1 h−1 with high Faradaic efficiency of 26.6 %.

Ferric chloride (0.5728 mmol), cobalt chloride (0.5728 mmol), nickel chloride (0.5728 mmol), manganese chloride (0.5728 mmol) and copper chloride (0.5728 mmol) were dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water. Dropped it into an aqueous solution containing 0.9864 g of SHB-GQD and stirred 3 h. To obtain MnFeCoNiCu2HEA/SHB-GQD, it was sucked into 1.644 g of cotton, dried at 60 ℃ in vacuum for 24 h and annealed at 900 ℃ for 4 h in an argon atmosphere containing 5 % H2 with a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1 in sequence. The above procedure was also used for preparation of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD, MnFeCoNiCu2HEA/H-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SH-GQD unless replace of SHB-GQD by GQD, H-GQD and SH-GQD, and preparation of MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/SHB-GQD unless varying molar ratio of Cu/Mn from 2.0 to 1.0 and 1.5.

Electrocatalytic urea synthesis was carried out on CHI830D workstation equipped with a H-cell system. H-cell system is composed of one carbon paper (1 cm × 1 cm) coated by MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD as working electrode, one standard Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode, and one platinum plate (1 cm × 1 cm) as counter electrode. One Nafion 117 membrane was used for the separation of cathode and anode chambers into. The working electrode was prepared by using following procedure: 2 mg of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD was dispersed in a mixed solution, containing 950 μL of ethanol, 50 μL of 5 wt% Nafion solution. The obtained solution was given a sonication treatment of 30 min to produce MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD ink. The ink of 20 μL was dropped on the surface of carbon paper (1 cm × 1 cm) and then dried in vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

The electrocatalytic urea synthesis adopts a 0.1 M KNO3 aqueous solution saturated by CO2 gas at ambient temperature. During the reaction, a constant CO2 flow rate of 30 mL min−1 was remained. The potential against Ag/AgCl reference electrode was changed into RHE reference scale by following formula: E (vs. RHE) = E (vs. Ag/AgCl) + 0.197 V + 0.0591 pH value (pH = 6.8). To avoid the interference from O2 in air, the electrolyte was purged with argon for at least 30 min before use.

DFT calculation was implemented by using a Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP). The interaction between atomic cores and valence electron density were described by the frozen-core projector augmented-wave (PAW) method. Within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA), the exchange-correlation potential was approximated using Perdew-Burke Ernzerhof (PBE) functional. The dispersion corrected DFT-D3 scheme was used to describe Van der Waals (vdW) interaction. The plane-wave cutoff energy was set to 500 eV. The conjugate gradient algorithm was used in the ionic optimization, and convergence threshold was set to 10−5 eV atom−1 in the electronic relaxation, and 0.02 eV Å−1 in Hellmann–Feynman force on each atom. The Brillouin zone in reciprocal space was treated by a Γ-centered Monkhorst-Pack scheme with 3 × 3 × 1 k-point grids to optimize the alloy model geometry. A 15 Å vacuum layer was added to the slab model to avoid the interference between adjacent configurations. A 20 Å × 20 Å × 20 Å cell was built to avoid the interaction of periodic boundary conditions [31].

Fig. 1 presents synthesis principle of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. The synthesis includes three important conversions. The first conversion is metal ion coordinates with SHB-GQD to form Me-SHB-GQD complex. SHB-GQD contains abundant functional groups such as -OH, -COOH, -NH- and imidazolyl, which can coordinate with metal ions to form stable complex. Due to excellent water-solubility of SHB-GQD and Me-SHB-GQD (shown in Fig. s1), each metal ion almost has the same chance of binding to graphene sheets. By the coordination, free metal ions and graphene sheets are combined into a complex network system. This will effectively prevent the separation of different metal ions each other caused by an obvious difference in the diffusion rate of metal ions. Further, Me-SHB-GQD solution was sucked into cotton and subsequently dried to remove free water molecules. The existence of strong coordination bond avoids the decomposition of Me-SHB-GQD during the drying. In addition, the introduction of cotton creates a spatial physical barrier between Me-SHB-GQD complexes. This leads to an improved dispersion of metal ions in the three-dimensional space. To confirm the suggest, atomically resolved elemental mapping was used to characterize the spatial distribution of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu elements. Fig. s2 indicates that all metal elements are well dispersed in space, showing an excellent dispersion. The above results demonstrate the conversion of metal ions into Me-SHB-GQD complex can ensure uniform distribution of all metal elements at atom level in a solution state and in a solid state. The second conversion is metal ion in Me-SHB-GQD was reduced into metal atom. At a high temperature, SHB-GQD and cotton will be decomposed to produce many reducing gases. These gases can reduce metal ion in Me-SHB-GQD complex to metal atom. The third conversion is metal atoms are alloyed to produce single-phase MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanocrystals. Unlike carbothermal shock method, our method does not appear a lot of metal atomic vapor, because the annealing temperature used is much lower than that of melting/boiling points of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu. Thus, it is not possible to achieve the alloying of metal elements by rapid collision of metal atom vapors. In our synthesis, the alloying is done with the help of the enormous energy generated by the explosion of gases from thermal decomposition of SHB-GQD and cotton. Carried by the gas slow, the metal atoms collide in space and eventually form MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanocrystals under the tendency of high entropy. The prerequisite for achieving this goal is that a large amount of reducing gases can be produced during the annealing. Herein, we performed a thermogravimetric analysis of the mixture of SHB-GQD and cotton. Fig. s3 shows a mass loss of more than 60 wt% occurs in the temperature rise from 50 °C to 800 °C. Such a large mass loss stems from the splitting of SHB-GQD and cotton into gases. These gases escape from the system and cause a large mass loss. The above result demonstrates the thermal annealing of Me-SHB-GQD complex/cotton can produce large amounts of gases. These gases will rapidly release form the system and induces an explosion in a confined space. These gases carry metal atoms to violently collide in space and leads to the formation of HEA nanoparticles. To confirm the suggest, the composition of gases produced by the pyrolysis of SHB-GQD/cotton at 370 °C and 600 °C was determined by GC-MS technique. Table s1 and Table s2 show the thermal annealing can produces many strong reducing gases such as furfuraldehyde and methacrolein. These gases can reduce metal ions into metal atoms at a high temperature, achieving the conversion of metal ions into metal atoms. In addition, the thermal annealing also produces large number of nitrogen-containing gases. Nitrogen atoms in these gases can coordinate with metal ions. The gas diffusion can carry metal atoms forward that favors the formation of HEA nanoparticles via the strong collision.

Fig. 2 presents SEM, TEM, HRTEM and HAADF-STEM of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD and element mappings of Ni, Cu, Co, Fe, Mn, C, O, B and merged elements. The SEM image reveals there is one three-dimensional carbon network (Fig. 2A). This is attributed to the carbonization of cotton during the thermal annealing. The presence of carbon network can accelerate the electron/electrolyte transfer for the electrocatalytic urea synthesis. The enlarged SEM image shows many white small spheres on the surface of carbon fiber (Fig. 2B), showing the presence of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanoparticles. The results of SEM and TEM analysis confirm MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanoparticle has one sphere-like morphology with an average particle size of 114 ± 1.2 nm (Fig. 2B). Further, the TEM images reveal a sphere shape is irregular (Fig. 2C). This could be attributed to the formation of copper-rich phase. There is one spacing of 0.194 nm on the HRTEM image, which corresponds to the (200) pane of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA (Fig. 2D). There are amorphous carbon (square region) and graphene sheets (rectangle region) on the HRTEM image (Fig. 2D). Graphene offers clear spot lattice of (002) pane with one spacing of 0.345 nm (upper in Fig. 2E), while amorphous carbon shows a ring pattern of (002) pane with a space of 0.381 nm (bottom in Fig. 2E). Clearly, graphene offers a narrower spacing compared with amorphous carbon. This owes to a better crystallinity of graphene. The HAADF-STEM and element mappings show the spatial distribution of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, C, O and B elements on the MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanoparticles (Fig. 2F-O). Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu elements only come from MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanoparticle. C and B only come from SHB-GQD. Thus, the results of element mapping analysis also demonstrate MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD is composed of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanoparticle and SHB-GQD. The element mappings of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu are consistent with the shape of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanoparticles. This verifies that MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD has a good chemical homogeneity. Further, ICP-OES technique was used for the determination of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu content in MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. The molar ratios of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu are 1: 1: 1:1: 2, which is consistent with the composition of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA. Based on total mass of all metal elements, the mass ratio of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA can be calculated. The result shows the mass ratio of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA is 23.81 wt% in MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD.

Fig. 3 A and B present FTIR spectrum and Raman spectrum of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. There are seven main IR absorption peaks and absorption bands at 3421, 2854, 1632, 1454, 1100 and 725 cm−1 on the FTIR MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD ( Fig. 3A). The IR absorption band at wavenumber between 4000 and 3300 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of O-H, N-H and unsaturated -C-H bonds. The IR absorption peak at the wavenumber of 2854 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of saturated C-H. The IR absorption band at wavenumber of about 1632 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of CO and CC bonds. The IR absorption peak at wavenumber of 1354 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of -O bond. The IR absorption peak at wavenumber of 1100 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of C-O-C bond. The IR absorption peak at wavenumber of 615 cm−1 is due to the out-of-plane bending vibration of -CH3 group. The above results demonstrate that the FTIR analysis demonstrate MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD contains -OH, -COOH, -NH- and -CH3. There are two Raman absorption peaks at 1335 cm−1 and 1617 cm−1 on the Raman spectrum of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD (Fig. 3B), which correspond to D peak and G peak of graphene [32]. The intensity ratio of D peak and G peak (ID/IG) was found to be 1.339. Such a high ID/IG reveals that graphene sheets offer a good dispersion on the MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD.

Fig. 3C and D present the XRD patterns and enlarged (111) diffraction peaks of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/SHB -GQD. There is one wide diffraction peak at about 25 ° between 10 and 40 ° (Fig. 3C). The peak could be assigned to the (002) pane of graphene. Due to a poor crystallinity, the diffraction peak of graphene shows a weak and wide feature. There are eight diffraction peaks at 43.4°, 43.8°, 50.5°, 51.0°, 74.2°, 75.1°, 90.2° and 91.3° on the XRD pattern of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/SHB-GQD. The four peaks at 43.4°, 50.5°, 74.2° and 90.2° can be assigned to the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) of FCC1 phase. The four peaks at 43.8°, 51.0°, 75.1° and 91.3° can be assigned to the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) of FCC2 phase. The above results confirm the as-proposed synthesis strategy can realize the production of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA with double FCC phase structure. Among FCC1 and FCC2 phases, Fig. s4 presents FCC1 phase is copper-rich phase [33]. With the increase in Cu ratio, the intensity of diffraction intensity in FCC1 phase will rapidly increase (Fig. 3D). Cu2+ is the easiest metal to reduce due to its relatively low reducing potential among five metal elements in HEA. The rapid accumulation of copper atoms leads to the formation of copper-rich phase, especially in the presence of a high proportion of copper.

Fig. 4 presents high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, Bs1, Mn2p, Fe2p, Co2p, Ni2p and Cu2p in MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s could be divided into four peaks at binding energy of 284.3, 285.9, 289.4 and 293.5 eV, which correspond to C-C, C-O, O-CO and COOR species [34]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s could be divided into three peaks at binding energy of 395.2, 396.9 and 403.1 eV, which correspond to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N species. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s could be divided into four peaks at binding energy of 530.9, 532.1, 533.5 and 536.1 eV, which correspond to CO, C-OH, OC-C and OC-OH species. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of B1s could be divided into three peaks at binding energy of 191.0, 192.3 and 193.4 eV, which correspond to BC3, BC2O and BCO2 species [35]. As C, N and B elements come from SHB-GQD and cotton, the above results of XPS analysis demonstrate that many functional groups in SHB-GQD and cotton can be remained during the thermal annealing. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mn2p could be divided into six peaks at binding energy of 636.8, 641.5, 643.9, 648.3, 653.7 and 659.2 eV. The two peaks at binding energy of 636.8 and 648.3 eV correspond to Mn2p3/2 and Mn2p1/2 of Mn0, showing the existence of Mn0 specie. The two peaks at binding energy of 641.5 and 653.7 eV correspond to Mn2p3/2 and Mn2p1/2 of Mn2+, showing the existence of Mn2+ specie. The two peaks at binding energy of 643.9 and 659.2 eV correspond to Mn2p3/2 and Mn2p1/2 of Mn3+, showing the existence of Mn3+ specie [36]. High-resolution XPS spectrum of Fe2p could be divided into four peaks at binding energy of 709.0, 715.6, 721.5 and 730.9 eV. The two peaks at binding energy of 709.0 and 721.5 eV correspond to Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 of Fe0, showing the existence of Fe0 specie. The two peaks at binding energy of 715.6 and 730.9 eV correspond to Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 of Fe2+, showing the existence of Fe2+ specie [37]. High-resolution XPS spectrum of Co2p could be divided into four peaks at binding energy of 777.4, 783.1, 792.9 and 799.6 eV. The two peaks at binding energy of 777.4 and 792.9 eV correspond to Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2 of Co0, showing the existence of Co0 specie. The two peaks at binding energy of 783.1 and 799.6 eV correspond to Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2 of Co2+, showing the existence of Co2+ specie [38]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ni2p could be divided into six peaks at binding energy of 851.4, 857.1, 862.6, 867.5, 872.7 and 878.9 eV. The two peaks at binding energy of 851.4 and 867.5 eV correspond to Ni2p3/2 and Ni2p1/2 of Ni0, showing the existence of Ni0 specie. The two peaks at binding energy of 857.1 and 872.7 eV correspond to Ni2p3/2 and Ni2p1/2 of Ni2+, showing the existence of Ni2+ specie. The two peaks at binding energy of 862.6 and 878.9 eV correspond to Ni2p3/2 and Ni2p1/2 of satellite Ni2p (Sat.), showing the existence of Sat. specie [39]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu2p could be divided into six peaks at binding energy of 928.7, 931.0, 938.6, 949.4, 954.1 and 958.9 eV. The two peaks at binding energy of 928.7 and 949.4 eV correspond to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 of Cu0, showing the existence of Cu0 specie. The two peaks at binding energy of 931.0 and 954.1 eV correspond to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 of Cu2+, showing the existence of Cu2+ specie. The two peaks at binding energy of 938.6 and 958.9 eV correspond to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 of satellite Cu2p (Sat.), showing the existence of Sat. specie [40]. The above results indicate the presence of zero-valent Mn, Fe, Ni, Co and Cu, verifying the formation of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA, and some high valent metal ions exist on the surface of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA nanoparticles. These metal ions are combined with SHB-GQD to stable modification layer. This significantly improves the structural stability of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA and affinity with polar electrolyte, leading to an improved electrocatalytic activity.

Urea synthesis was employed as one model reaction for evaluating the electrocatalytic performance of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. Fig. 5A shows the LSV curves of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD electrode at the potential between −1.3 and 0.1 V in the Ar- (or CO2)-saturated 0.1 M KNO3 (or K2SO4) electrolyte. The LSV current density is close to zero in Ar-saturated 0.1 K2SO4 electrolyte when the potential is lower than −0.9 V, showing no redox of Ar and SO4 2- occur on the electrode. However, the current density slightly increases with the increase of potential when the potential is more than −0.9 V. This is due to the reduction of H+ ions in the electrolyte on the electrode surface. Due to the existence of rich metal cations, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD will strongly repel H+ ions close to the electrode surface. This characteristic makes H+ ion offers a very high overpotential on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD electrode. High overpotential prevents the reduction of H+ ions and results in a very low current density. Because the electron transfer occurring during NO3RR was more frequent than that of CO2RR, the current increase in K2SO4/CO2 was lower than that in KNO3/Ar. The above results demonstrate that MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD can catalyze the reduction of NO3 - and CO2. When the CO2 - saturated 0.1 M KNO3 solution was used as the electrolyte for the test, the current density reaches to the maximum. This is attributed to the combination of CO2 reduction with NO3 - reduction to form urea, forming C-N bond. In addition, Fig. 5A also indicates the current density is lower than zero when the potential is equal to 0.1 V, verifying urea synthesis can carry out at the potential of 0.1 V. A low reduction potential demonstrates MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD has an excellent electrocatalytic activity for urea synthesis.

Fig. 5B, C and D present the i-t curves, urea yields and Faraday efficiencies of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD electrode at −0.3, −0.4, −0.5, −0.6 and −0.7 V vs. the RHE in the CO2-saturated 0.1 M KNO3 electrolyte for 60 min. At all potentials used, the current density is lower than zero, showing CO2 and NO3 - can be reduced at the potential between −0.3 and −0.7 V. With the increase of potential, the reduction of CO2 and NO3 - was accelerated and leads to an increased current density (Fig. 5B). However, the changes in urea yields and Faraday efficiencies do not follow this pattern. The urea yield rapidly increases when the potential is lower than −0.5 V (Fig. 5C and Fig. S5), reaches to the maximum (59.01 mmol gcatalyst −1) with the highest Faraday efficiency (26.6 %) at the potential of −0.5 V (Fig. 5D), and then decreases. To obtain high urea yield and avoid serious side effects, a potential of −0.5 V was selected as a working potential for electrocatalytic urea synthesis under catalysis of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD.

Fig. 5E and F present the i-t curves, urea yields and Faraday efficiencies values (FEs) for 60 min of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD electrode in CO2-saturated 0.1 M KNO3 electrolyte at −0.5 V for 8-cycle test. It can be seen from Fig. 5E all i-t curves give a very similar shape and current density during 8-cycle test. This demonstrates the reduce in electroactive components caused by electrode material detachment as well as side reactions can be almost ignored. The urea yield remains 56.74 mmol gcatalyst −1 after 8-cycle test with a FE of 26.33 %. This result shows that 8 cycles only bring about a 3.85 %reduce in the urea yield and a 1.02 % reduce in the FE value.

FE values for electrocatalytic preparation of CO, H2, NO2 -, NH3 and urea in a CO2-saturated 0.1 M KNO3 electrolyte at −0.5 V for 60 min on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD catalyst at −0.5 V were presented at Fig. 6, Fig. s6 and Fig. s7. The FE value for synthesis of urea is up to 26.60 %, which is more than for H2 (2.53 %), NH3 (20.49 %) and CO (8.03 %). Although FE value is 42.14 % that is higher than that for urea, it can be converted into urea in further reduction. The above result demonstrates that the urea synthesis on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD offers a good selectivity.

To evaluate the effect of high entropy on catalytic activity, five electrocatalysts, including Mn/SHB-GQD, MnFe/SHB-GQD, MnFeCo/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNi/SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD, were made and used for urea synthesis by using the same procedure. Fig. 7 presents the urea yield for electrocatalytic urea synthesis in CO2-saturated 0.1 M KNO3 electrolyte at −0.5 V for 60 min on the Mn/SHB-GQD, MnFe/SHB-GQD, MnFeCo/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNi/SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD electrodes. The urea yields are 4.47 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 of Mn/SHB-GQD, 15.32 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 of MnFe/SHB-GQD, 20.84 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 of MnFeCo/SHB-GQD, 32.58 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 of MnFeCoNi/SHB-GQD and 59.01 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. These data demonstrate that the introduction of each metal element brings an improved urea yield, showing an enhanced electrocatalytic activity. Among five electrocatalysts used, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA /SHB-GQD offers the best electrocatalytic activity. Such an excellent catalytic activity is attributed to the high entropy effect arising from the mixture of Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu.

For the comparison, the electrocatalytic behaviors of reported electrocatalysts for urea synthesis were listed in Table 1. Table 1 shows MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD can offer a better electrocatalytic performance compared with other electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of SHB-GQD on the electrocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: Four electrocatalysts, including MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/ SH-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/H-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD, were synthesized and used for the electrocatalytic urea synthesis to evaluate the effect of SHB-GQD on the electrocatalytic activity. Fig. 8A presents the urea yields in a CO2-saturated 0.1 M KNO3 electrolyte at −0.5 V for 60 min on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SH-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/ H-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD. The urea yield depends on the structure of GQD. The urea yield of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD electrode reaches to 59.01 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1, showing an excellent catalytic activity. The value is obviously higher than that of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SH-GQD (40.52 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1), MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/H-GQD (33.28 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1) and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD (18.09 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1). These data reveal the introductions of histidine, serine and boron bring 15.19, 7.24 and 18.49 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 can achieve to an increase on urea yield. More importantly, the above result gives us one new insight for improving the electrocatalytic activity by rational design of GQD.

To understand the above improvement, the semiconductor properties of GQD, H-GQD, SH-GQD and SHB-GQD were investigated by UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum technique. Fig. 8B indicates a different GQD exhibits a different UV–visible diffuse reflectance behavior. GQD shows one strong light absorption capacity towards ultraviolet light and one weak light absorption capacity towards visible light, especially visible light with a wavelength of more than 620 nm. The introduction of histidine (H-GQD) brings an obvious increase in light absorption capacity towards ultraviolet light and visible light. However, the H-GQD still indicate a very weak light absorption capacity towards visible light with the wavelength of more than 620 nm. Interestingly, the capacity was significantly improved by the introduction of serine. SH-GQD shows one strong light absorption capacity towards visible light with wavelength of more than 620 nm. This capacity was further strengthened with the introduction of boron atom. SHB-GQD exhibits the best absorption capacity towards visible light with wavelength of more than 620 nm. Further, the plots of the transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs. the energy of light of GQD, H-GQD, SH-GQD and SHB-GQD were shown in Fig. 8B. By extending the tangent of the curve to intersect with the horizontal axis, the bandgap energy (Eg) of semiconductor material can be determined. The results shows that GQD gives a relatively big Eg value of 2.43 eV, showing a low electrical conductivity. The Eg value will reduce to 2.36 eV of H-GQD, 2.22 eV of SH-GQD and 2.06 eV of SHB-GQD. This confirms the introduction of histidine, serine and boron leads to a reduced Eg value. Since GQD is one semiconductor and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA is conductor, the combination of GQD with MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA induces the production of Schottky heterojunctions at the interface. This promotes the electron transfer at the interface and leads to an improved electrocatalytic activity. A GQD with a smaller Eg value achieves to a faster electron transfer process at the interface, showing a better catalytic activity. As a result, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD offers the best catalytic activity for urea synthesis.

To confirm the above suggest, LSV curves of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu2.0 HEA/SH-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/H-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD electrodes in the CO2-saturated 0.1 M KNO3 electrolyte at the potential between −1.3 and 0.1 V were measured and the results were shown in Fig. 8D. For MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD, its LSV current density is close to zero when the potential is lower than −0.57 V, showing urea synthesis cannot carry out at the potential of lower than −0.57 V. However, the LSV current density rapidly increases with the increase of reduction potential, showing a fast urea synthesis process. For the convenience, the minimum potential required to synthesize urea was named as the starting potential (Es). A relatively high Es value confirms that MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD only can offer a low electrocatalytic activity towards urea synthesis. The Es value reduces to −0.43 V when MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD was replaced by MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/H-GQD, verifying that MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/H-GQD gives a better electrocatalytic activity compared with MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/GQD. The Es value further decreases to 0.0041 V when MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/H-GQD was replaced by MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA /SH-GQD, showing a significantly enhanced electrocatalytic activity. Among four electrocatalysts, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD has the smallest Es value (0.1 V), showing the best electrocatalytic activity.

Based on the above results, we suggest the introduction of GQD narrows the bandgap. This accelerates the electron transfer at the interface and leads to an improved electrocatalytic activity for urea synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of copper-rich phase on the electrocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: Three electrocatalysts, including MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA /SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0 HEA/SHB-GQD, were synthesized and used for electrocatalytic urea synthesis by using the same procedure to evaluate the effect of copper-rich phase on the electrocatalytic activity. Fig. 9 shows different catalyst gives an obvious difference in electrocatalytic activity. The urea yields are 28.16 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 of MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/SHB-GQD, 37.81 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 of MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/ SHB-GQD and 59.01 mmol gcatalyst −1 h−1 of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. These data demonstrate MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD offers the best catalytic activity for urea synthesis among three electrocatalysts. Since only difference between three electrocatalysts is the difference in the atomic ratio of copper, the above improvement in electrocatalytic activity could be attributed to the existence of copper-rich phase in MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD.

To understand the above improvement, the CV behaviors of MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/ SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0 HEA/SHB-GQD electrodes were investigated to measure the electrochemical surface areas (ECSAs). Fig. 10 shows MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/SHB-GQD gives the lowest CV current density among three electrodes, verifying the presence of the least active sites. The CV current density is much lower than that of MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD electrodes at all scan rates. This result demonstrates the increase on copper ratio in HEA causes an increase in electrochemically active sites. Among three electrodes studied, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD offers the highest CV current density, showing the presence of the most electrochemical active sites. Furthermore, the relationships between the change in current density (ΔJ) and the scan rates for MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/SHB-GQD, MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/SHB-GQD and MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD electrodes were shown in Fig. 10D. The ΔJ value indicates a good linear relationship with the scan rate. The corresponding slopes (Cdl) are 48.91 mF cm−2 of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD, 22.94 mF cm−2 of MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/SHB-GQD, and 13.21 mF cm−2 of MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/ SHB-GQD. For similar nanomaterials, the Cdl value can represent the ECSA of the catalyst [65]. Thus, the above data confirm the ECSA of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD is more than 2.1-fold that of MnFeCoNiCu1.5HEA/SHB-GQD and 3.7-fold that of MnFeCoNiCu1.0HEA/SHB-GQD.
Based on the results, we suggest the formation of copper-rich phase in HEA nanoparticles creates more electrochemically active sites. This leads to an improved electrocatalytic activity.

The in-situ FTIR spectroscopies on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD at potentials of −0.3, −0.4, −0.5, −0.6 and −0.7 V were used for verifying the formation of important chemical bonds in urea synthesis during the electrocatalytic coupling process of NO3 - and CO2. Fig. 11 shows the in-situ FTIR spectroscopies include several IR absorption peaks at the wavenumber range of 1000–4000 cm−1. The peak at 1669 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of CO bond. The two peak at 1641 and 1162 cm−1 are caused by the bending and rocking frequencies of -NH2 in urea. The peak at 1416 cm−1 is the IR absorption caused by stretching vibration of C-N bond, verifying the co-activation and coupling of CO2 and NO3 - occurs in the urea synthesis. The peak at 1397 cm−1 is the IR absorption caused by vibrational band of OCO bond. The occurrence of C-N and OCO bonds demonstrates the existence of CO2NO2 intermediate, which is converted into CO2NH2. The peak at 2296 cm−1 is due to the asymmetric vibration of CO2, showing CO2 can be firmly adsorbed on the surface of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. The peak at 3445 cm−1 is due to stretching vibration of N-H bond, verifying the presence of HOCONH2, which shed -OH to form CONH2 specie. The CONH2 is one reactant in 2-nd C-N coupling. The peaks at 3301 cm−1 is attributed to the emergence of H-N-H stretching mode and N-H bending mode, showing the existence of CONH2. The two peaks at 1641 and 1162 cm−1 are IR absorption caused by bending mode and rocking mode of amino. After 2nd C-N coupling electrocatalytic reactions on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD, urea molecule (H2NCONH2) is formed. This exposes the special bending mode and rocking mode of amino at 1641 and 1161 cm−1. In general, the results of in-situ FTIR analysis demonstrate that MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD can catalyze the reduction of NO3 - and CO2 to produce urea.

The isotopically labeled nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) technique was employed as one tool to explore the origin of N and C elements in the urea product. Fig. s8A shows the 1H NMR spectra of standard urea (14N-urea) and 15N isotopic labeled urea (15N-urea), and the urea products catalyzed by MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD in 0.1 M KNO3 (K14NO3) saturated with CO2 and in 0.1 M 15N isotopically labeled KNO3 (K15NO3) saturated with CO2. The 1H NMR spectrum of 14N-urea offers one broad peak at the chemical shift of 5.48 ppm, while the 1H NMR spectrum of 15N-urea shows two sharp peaks at 5.58 and 5.43 ppm. This obvious difference allows for the precise identification of 14N-urea and 15N-urea in a mixed system. Furthermore, Fig. s8A also shows the 1H NMR spectrum of urea product in 0.1 M K15NO3 saturated with CO2 matches that of standard 15N-urea, with no any characteristic 1H NMR peak of 14N-urea at 5.48 ppm. This demonstrates the N in urea product originates only from K15NO3. Fig. s8B shows the 13C NMR spectra for standard 13C isotopically labeled urea (13C-urea) and the urea product synthesized by MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD in 0.1 M KNO3 saturated with 13C isotopically labeled CO2 (13CO2). The 13C NMR spectrum of the standard 13C-urea is consistent with that of the urea product. The result confirms the C in the urea product originates only from CO2 in the reaction system.

The reaction mechanism for electrocatalytic urea synthesis was also studied by DFT calculation. Fig. 12A presents the structural model of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. The structure was stochastically modeled by using the SQS approach. The alloy structure gives one Fm3m space group basing on the element composition in the ratio of Cu: Fe: Co: Ni: Mn = 2.0:1:1:1:1. The simulation of the adsorption process is built on the (001) surface [42]. Free energies and the structural evolution of active intermediate at this site were shown in Fig. 12B-E and Fig. s9-s14 . The free energies for adsorption of CO2, NO3 - and H+ are −0.65 eV for H+, −1.13 eV for CO2, and −2.09 eV for NO3 - (Fig. 12B). All free energies are negative, verifying the adsorption of CO2 NO3 - and H+ on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD are thermodynamic spontaneous processes. This facilitates the electrocatalytic reduction of CO2 and NO3 - into urea. The adsorption of H+ causes a low free energy change (-0.65 eV), showing a poor affinity between MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD and H+. This results in a high overpotential for the reduction of H+. Different from H+, the adsorptions of CO2 or NO3 - produces a high free energy change (-1.13 eV or 2.09 eV), showing a good affinity between MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD and CO2 or NO3 -. This is beneficial to the reduction of CO2 or NO3 - on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. More importantly, an obvious difference in adsorption free energy of H+, CO2 and NO3 - significantly improves the selectivity for urea synthesis.

The adsorbed CO2 is converted into *CO (Fig. 12C). The conversion includes three steps, forming *CO2, *COOH and *CO intermediates. The adsorbed CO2 (*CO2) captures one H+ from the electrolyte and one electron from the electrode to form one *COOH. Then, it strips away one OH and captures two electrons from the electrode to form one *CO. The above reaction steps give a negative free energy change, showing the production of *COOH and *CO are thermodynamic spontaneous processes. Thus, *CO2 is easy to be changed into *CO on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD.

The adsorbed NO3 - is converted into *NH2 (Fig. 12D). The conversion includes eight steps, forming *NO3, *NO2, *NOOH, *NO, *NHO, *NHOH, *NH, and *NH2 intermediates. Unless *NO3 and *NO2, the production of *NOOH, *NO, *NHO, *NHOH, *NH, and *NH2 needs to capture H+ from the electrolyte and electron from the electrode. In the eight steps, the productions of *NO3, *NO2, *NO, *NHO, *NHOH, *NH and *NH2 are the process of decreasing free energy increasing fee energy. Thus, their formations are reactions are thermodynamic spontaneous processes. This demonstrates that the production of *NOOH only needs to overcome a small energy barrier to be performed. Thus, *NO3 - is easy to be converted into *NH2 under the electrocatalysis of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD.

Fig. 12B, C and D also shows all reaction steps on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD have an obviously reduced free energy compared with that on MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA. The result demonstrates that the introduction of SHB-GQD brings an improved electrocatalytic activity towards the adsorption of CO2 NO3 - and H+ and reduction of CO2 and NO3 -.

The formed *CO and *NH2 are reduced to urea molecule (*CO(NH2)2) via 2nd C-N coupling reaction under the electrocatalysis of MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD. The coupling of *NH2 and *CO forms one *CONH2 intermediate. In the process, the formation of transition state (TS) requires overcoming a small energy barrier of 0.96 eV. However, the intermediate is converted to *CO(NH2)2 by an exothermic proces. This is a spontaneous step with a downhill energy step with the ΔG of −2.33 eV. Many researches have confirmed that the C-N coupling is the most difficult conversion to occur in electrocatalytic urea synthesis. But the conversion only needs one jumping over a small energy barrier and then releasing a large amount of energy to complete. Therefore, MnFeCoNiCu2.0HEA/SHB-GQD can efficiently catalyze the reduction of NO3 - and CO2 into urea.

Mild synthesis of MnFeCoNiCu2 high-entropy alloy nanoparticle has been demonstrated. The study reveals strong coordination between serine and histidine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot and metal ions ensures an uniform distribution of all metal elements at atom level in a solution state and in a solid state. This effectively suppresses the structural heterogeneity of high-entropy alloy and results in the formation of high-entropy alloy nanoparticles with double FCC phase. The introduction of functional graphene quantum dot also narrows the bandgap and leads to an improved conductivity. The introduction of copper-rich phase increases the electrochemically active sites. The integration of functional graphene quantum dot with copper-rich phase significantly enhances the electrocatalytic activity. The as-synthesized high-entropy alloy nanoparticle as an electrocatalyst for urea synthesis provides ultrahigh urea yield of 59.01 mmol g−1 h−1 with high Faradaic efficiency of 26.6 %. The study also offers one new insight to make various high-entropy alloy nanoparticles with a desired electrocatalytic performance by rational design of GQD.

Li Zaijun: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Li Ruiyi: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Liu Xiaohao: Investigation. Zhang Qingqing: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in the synthesis and fabrication methods of high-entropy alloy nanoparticles
